**Buddhists**, also known as ***Bauddha***, are people who adhere to [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), an [Indian religious](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions") and [philosophical tradition](/wiki/Indian_philosophy "Indian philosophy") founded by [the Buddha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha") in the 6th or 5th century [BCE](/wiki/BCE "BCE") in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). Buddhist identity may be religious, cultural, or secular, encompassing ordained members of the [monastic community](/wiki/Buddhist_monasticism "Buddhist monasticism") (*[Saṅgha](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%85gha "Saṅgha")*), [lay practitioners](/wiki/Householder_(Buddhism) "Householder (Buddhism)"), and individuals who adopt Buddhist ethical principles, meditation, or mindfulness practices without formal ritual affiliation.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

Buddhists are broadly classified into two main categories: [Bhikkhus](/wiki/Bhikkhu "Bhikkhu") and [Bhikkhunīs](/wiki/Bhikkhun%C4%AB "Bhikkhunī"), who are ordained monastics and serve as religious teachers, and [Upāsakas](/wiki/Up%C4%81saka "Upāsaka") and [Upāsikās](/wiki/Up%C4%81sik%C4%81 "Upāsikā"), who are lay followers living a household life while supporting and practicing the teachings of Buddhism.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

In ancient India, followers of the Buddha were sometimes identified as [Shakyas](/wiki/Shakya "Shakya"), after the clan into which the Buddha was born.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The English word *Buddhist* emerged in the early 19th century, formed from "Buddha" and the [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") "-ist", denoting an adherent of a belief system; its earliest recorded usage in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") dates to 1810.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Buddhism comprises numerous [schools and traditions](/wiki/Schools_of_Buddhism "Schools of Buddhism"), alongside [non-denominational](/wiki/Secular_Buddhism "Secular Buddhism") and secular forms. The two largest traditions are [Theravāda Buddhism](/wiki/Therav%C4%81da_Buddhism "Theravāda Buddhism"), predominant in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Sri_Lanka "Buddhism in Sri Lanka"), [Thailand](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Thailand "Buddhism in Thailand"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Myanmar "Buddhism in Myanmar"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Cambodia "Buddhism in Cambodia"), and [Laos](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Laos "Buddhism in Laos"), and [Mahayana Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism "Mahayana Buddhism"), which is dominant in [China](/wiki/Buddhism_in_China "Buddhism in China"), [Japan](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Japan "Buddhism in Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/Korean_Buddhism "Korean Buddhism"), and [Vietnam](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Vietnam "Buddhism in Vietnam").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

There are approximately 530 million Buddhists worldwide, accounting for about 7% of the global population.[[8]](#cite_note-auto-8) Nevertheless, Buddhism has gained cultural and intellectual influence in [Western countries](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), driven in part by growing interest in [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation") and [mindfulness](/wiki/Mindfulness "Mindfulness") practices.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

By absolute numbers, Thailand constitutes the largest share of the global Buddhist population,[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3) followed by China and Myanmar.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Outside of Buddhist-majority countries, sizable Buddhist populations are found in [India](/wiki/History_of_Buddhism_in_India "History of Buddhism in India") and the [United States](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_United_States "Buddhism in the United States").[[11]](#cite_note-:52-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word "Buddhist" is derived from "Buddha", meaning "the awakened one", and was first used in English in the early 19th century to describe followers of the Buddha. In local contexts, early Indian practitioners were called *[Sramanas](/wiki/Sramana "Sramana")* (ascetics) or *Shramaneras* (novice monks), emphasizing renunciation and ethical practice.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) With the spread of Buddhism across Asia, terms like *Fo-zu* (China), *Bukkyōsha* (Japan), and *Chos-pa* (Tibet) came into use, reflecting local languages and cultural adaptations.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Terminology")]

> Monks, even if bandits were to carve you up savagely into pieces with a two-handled saw, he among you who let his heart get angered even at that would not be [a Buddhist].

[Buddha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha"), *Kakacūpama Sutta ([MN](/wiki/Majjhima_Nik%C4%81ya "Majjhima Nikāya") 21)*[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEṬhānissaro_Bhikkhu_MN_21-15)

The term "Buddhist" is used broadly to describe anyone who follows the teachings of the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha"), regardless of specific school or region. While the umbrella term "Buddhist" is widely recognized in [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") and other [European languages](/wiki/European_languages "European languages"), historical and regional variations exist. In early [Indian](/wiki/India "India") texts, practitioners were often called *[Sramanas](/wiki/Sramana "Sramana")*, meaning ascetics, or *[Shramanas](/wiki/Shramana "Shramana")* (novice monks), highlighting their focus on [ethical conduct](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"), [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation"), and [renunciation](/wiki/Renunciation "Renunciation").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

With the spread of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") across [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), different terms emerged to reflect local languages and traditions. In [China](/wiki/China "China"), followers were known as *Fo-zu* (Buddha followers) or *Fanfo* (foreign Buddha).[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) In [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), they were called *Bukkyōsha* (practitioners of the Buddha's teachings).[[16]](#cite_note-:3-16) [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and [Himalayan](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") communities use the term *Chos-pa* to denote those following the [Dharma](/wiki/Dharma_(Buddhism) "Dharma (Buddhism)").[[17]](#cite_note-:2-17)

Additionally, within Buddhist schools, specific identifiers exist: [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhists follow the [Pāli Canon](/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon "Pāli Canon") and [Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") (monastic discipline) primarily in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"); [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") Buddhists follow a diverse set of [Mahayana sutras](/wiki/Mahayana_sutras "Mahayana sutras") and emphasize the [Bodhisattva](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") path, particularly in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"); and [Vajrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") Buddhists engage in [esoteric practices](/wiki/Esoteric_Buddhism "Esoteric Buddhism") and rituals found mainly in [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), and [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Modern usage of the term "Buddhist" encompasses both traditional religious practitioners and secular individuals who adopt aspects of [Buddhist philosophy](/wiki/Buddhist_philosophy "Buddhist philosophy"), [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation"), and [ethical teachings](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") without necessarily adhering to [rituals](/wiki/Rituals "Rituals") or [monastic life](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism").[[19]](#cite_note-auto1-19)

## History of Buddhist identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History of Buddhist identity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/004_Daily_Life_%289140818127%29.jpg/250px-004_Daily_Life_%289140818127%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:004_Daily_Life_(9140818127).jpg)

Artwork depicting the scenes of Buddhists' daily life, [Tumpat](/wiki/Tumpat_District "Tumpat District"), Malaysia

[Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") identity has evolved over more than two [millennia](/wiki/Millennium "Millennium"), shaped by religious, cultural, and political contexts. In its earliest stages in [India](/wiki/India "India"), followers were identified primarily as *[Sramanas](/wiki/Sramana "Sramana")*—ascetics committed to [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation"), [ethical living](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"), and renunciation—rather than as "Buddhists."[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) After the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha")'s death, [Buddhist communities](/wiki/Buddhist_monasticism "Buddhist monasticism") organized around [viharas](/wiki/Vihara "Vihara"), and identity was often tied to adherence to the [Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") (monastic code) and the [Pāli Canon](/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon "Pāli Canon") or [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") scriptures.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

As [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") spread beyond India along [trade routes](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") to [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), regional and cultural adaptations influenced the formation of distinct Buddhist identities. In [China](/wiki/China "China"), Buddhism integrated with [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") and [Daoism](/wiki/Daoism "Daoism"), leading to the emergence of [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") traditions, while in [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), [Vajrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") practices became central, creating a [Tibetan Buddhist](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism") identity that combined religious, cultural, and political elements.[[17]](#cite_note-:2-17) In [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism became closely linked with national identity in countries such as [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), where kings and governments historically patronized [monasteries](/wiki/Buddhist_monasteries "Buddhist monasteries") and promoted [moral](/wiki/Buddhist_ethics "Buddhist ethics") and social values.[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)

During [colonial periods](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), European scholars and administrators began to categorize followers of Buddhism as "Buddhists," standardizing the term in global discourse. This classification sometimes contrasted with local identities, which were often based on [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity"), [region](/wiki/Region "Region"), or monastic affiliation rather than the broad religious label.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) In the modern era, Buddhist identity can be [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), cultural, or even secular, encompassing traditional [monastic practitioners](/wiki/Buddhist_monasticism "Buddhist monasticism"), lay devotees, and individuals who adopt [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation") and [ethical practices](/wiki/Buddhist_ethics "Buddhist ethics") without participating in formal [rituals](/wiki/Rituals "Rituals").[[19]](#cite_note-auto1-19)

## Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Origin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/071_Lay_and_Monastics_Worship_the_Buddha.jpg/250px-071_Lay_and_Monastics_Worship_the_Buddha.jpg)](/wiki/File:071_Lay_and_Monastics_Worship_the_Buddha.jpg)

Lay devotees and monastics paying homage to the [Buddha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Buddhist_Monk_at_Angkor_Wat_1.jpg/250px-Buddhist_Monk_at_Angkor_Wat_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buddhist_Monk_at_Angkor_Wat_1.jpg)

Buddhist monks at [Angkor Wat](/wiki/Angkor_Wat "Angkor Wat"), a significant Buddhist site in Cambodia

The origins of Buddhists as a distinct community can be traced to the northeastern [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") during the 6th to 4th centuries BCE, a period marked by social transformation, urbanisation, and philosophical inquiry. Early followers of the [Buddha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha") formed both monastic and lay communities, embracing his teachings on the [Middle Way](/wiki/Middle_Way "Middle Way"), [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths"), and [Noble Eightfold Path](/wiki/Noble_Eightfold_Path "Noble Eightfold Path").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Early Buddhists included members of the [Shakya](/wiki/Shakya "Shakya") clan, merchants, peasants, and other ascetic seekers, who were drawn to the Buddha's teachings as an alternative to ritualistic and hierarchical [Pali canon](/wiki/Pali_canon "Pali canon") traditions. The monastic community, or [Sangha](/wiki/Sangha "Sangha"), became central to Buddhist identity. Monks and nuns observed the [Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") (monastic code) and acted as custodians of the teachings, while lay Buddhists supported the Sangha through donations and practiced ethical precepts and meditation.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Buddhism originated in Nepal because Siddhārtha Gautama,the Buddha was born in Lumbini, in present-day Nepal, as a prince of the Shakya clan. From this Nepali origin, his spiritual quest and enlightenment gave rise to Buddhism, which later spread far beyond Nepal across Asia and the world.As Buddhism spread, distinct regional traditions and schools of thought developed, influencing the cultural and religious identity of Buddhists. [Theravāda Buddhism](/wiki/Therav%C4%81da_Buddhism "Theravāda Buddhism") became established in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") and Southeast Asia, emphasizing Pāli Canon study and monastic discipline, while [Mahayana Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism "Mahayana Buddhism") flourished in [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), incorporating local practices, devotional rituals, and philosophical innovations.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Today, this syncretic character is clearly visible in Nepal, particularly in the religious life of the Urāy (Urya) communities of the Kathmandu Valley and among the Buddhist Newar people, whose practices seamlessly blend Buddhist and Hindu philosophies, rituals, and deities. This harmony is also reflected in the deep reverence shown toward the Buddha by Hindus across Nepal. In many Hindu temples, the Buddha is honored and worshipped as an incarnation of Lord Vishnu, illustrating Nepal’s long-standing tradition of religious coexistence and mutual respect between Buddhism and Hinduism.

Buddhists began to influence [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") thought during the 19th and 20th centuries, particularly through the translation of [Buddhist texts](/wiki/Buddhist_texts "Buddhist texts"), scholarly study, and the interest of philosophers, psychologists, and writers. Western adherents often emphasize meditation, mindfulness, and ethical principles over ritual practice, reflecting a philosophical or secular approach to Buddhist identity.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In the 20th century, the establishment of Buddhist centers and monasteries in Europe and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") helped form new Buddhist communities. Figures such as [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama"), [Thich Nhat Hanh](/wiki/Thich_Nhat_Hanh "Thich Nhat Hanh") and [Chögyam Trungpa](/wiki/Ch%C3%B6gyam_Trungpa "Chögyam Trungpa") contributed to the adaptation of Buddhist teachings in Western cultural contexts, emphasizing mindfulness, meditation, and socially [engaged Buddhism](/wiki/Engaged_Buddhism "Engaged Buddhism").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Western Buddhists often integrate Buddhist practices with psychology, health, and well-being initiatives, including [Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction](/wiki/Mindfulness-Based_Stress_Reduction "Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction") (MBSR) and related programs. This modern interpretation has led to a pluralistic and globalized Buddhist identity, combining traditional spiritual teachings with secular ethical and meditative practices.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![China40.5/](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Buddhist_world_map_in_2020.png/330px-Buddhist_world_map_in_2020.png)](/wiki/File:Buddhist_world_map_in_2020.png)

Percentage of Buddhists by country in 2020, according to the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center")

As of 2020, there are approximately 530 million Buddhist worldwide, representing about 7% of the global population.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The majority of Buddhists reside in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), with significant populations in [China](/wiki/China "China"),[[29]](#cite_note-:7-29) [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"),[[30]](#cite_note-shukyo23-30) [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Thailand is home to the largest number of Buddhists, estimated at 67 million,[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3) including both practicing [monks](/wiki/Buddhist_monks "Buddhist monks") and lay followers who observe Buddhist customs and [Buddhist rituals](/wiki/Buddhist_rituals "Buddhist rituals").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") has substantial Buddhist populations, with [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") accounting for approximately 67 million, [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") 38 million, [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") 15 million,[[35]](#cite_note-Census-35) and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") 14 million.[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21) [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") also has significant Buddhist communities, including [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") with around 45 million followers and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") with about 11 million adherents.[[16]](#cite_note-:3-16)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") is practiced by people of diverse ethnic and cultural backgrounds.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), Buddhists are predominantly found among the [Sinhalese](/wiki/Sinhalese_people "Sinhalese people") in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), the [Bamar](/wiki/Bamar_people "Bamar people") in [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), and various minority groups in [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[38]](#cite_note-38) In [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people"), [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people"), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people "Vietnamese people"), [Thai](/wiki/Thai_people "Thai people"), and other ethnic groups maintain Buddhist traditions.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Outside [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), the global Buddhist population includes converts and practitioners in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and other regions, reflecting a growing secular or meditation-oriented form of Buddhist identity.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Buddhist culture](/wiki/Buddhist_culture "Buddhist culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Yee_Peng_Sansai_Thailand%2C_Buddhist_culture_religion_rites_rituals_sights.jpg/330px-Yee_Peng_Sansai_Thailand%2C_Buddhist_culture_religion_rites_rituals_sights.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yee_Peng_Sansai_Thailand,_Buddhist_culture_religion_rites_rituals_sights.jpg)

Buddhism ceremony celebrated with [sky lanterns](/wiki/Sky_lanterns "Sky lanterns"), Thailand

[Buddhist culture](/wiki/Buddhist_culture "Buddhist culture") encompasses a wide range of practices, beliefs, artistic expressions, and social institutions that have developed over more than two millennia. Central to [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") culture are [monastic communities](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism"), [meditation practices](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation"), [ethical conduct](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"), and [ritual observances](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual").[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) [Monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") serve as centers of learning, spiritual guidance, and community service, often playing an important role in [Education](/wiki/Education "Education"), [Healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare "Healthcare"), and social welfare in [Buddhist-majority countries](/wiki/List_of_Buddhists "List of Buddhists").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[Buddhist literature](/wiki/Buddhist_literature "Buddhist literature") includes canonical texts such as the [Pāli Canon](/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon "Pāli Canon"), [Mahayana sutras](/wiki/Mahayana_sutras "Mahayana sutras"), and commentaries, which provide guidance on philosophy, ethics, meditation, and ritual. Local traditions have produced extensive literature in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibetan_language "Tibetan language"), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), and other languages.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) [Meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation") practices, including [mindfulness (sati)](/wiki/Mindfulness "Mindfulness"), [concentration (samadhi)](/wiki/Samadhi "Samadhi"), and [Vipassana](/wiki/Vipassana "Vipassana") (insight), form an essential part of daily life for many Buddhists, whether monastic or lay, and have influenced global secular [mindfulness movements](/wiki/Mindfulness "Mindfulness").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Buddhist culture also emphasizes [Compassion](/wiki/Compassion "Compassion"), [nonviolence](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa"), and ethical living, often reflected in social and charitable activities.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In many countries, Buddhist organizations provide humanitarian aid, educational programs, and disaster relief, integrating spiritual teachings with practical service to society.[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)

### Buddhist festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Buddhist festivals")]

Main article: [Buddhist festivals](/wiki/Buddhist_festivals "Buddhist festivals")

Buddhist festivals are spiritual and cultural celebrations that commemorate important events in the life of the [Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha"), honor his teachings ([Dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma")), and strengthen the Buddhist community ([Sangha](/wiki/Sangha "Sangha")). While the dates and customs vary among different traditions—[Theravāda](/wiki/Therav%C4%81da "Theravāda"), [Mahāyāna](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na "Mahāyāna"), and [Vajrayāna](/wiki/Vajray%C4%81na "Vajrayāna")—all share a spirit of mindfulness, compassion, and gratitude.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Most are observed according to the lunar calendar and combine religious devotion with social harmony and acts of generosity.

#### Pan-Buddhist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Pan-Buddhist")]

The most widely observed Buddhist festival is [Vesak](/wiki/Vesak "Vesak") (also known as Buddha Day or Buddha Purnima). It celebrates the birth, enlightenment, and [passing into Nirvana](/wiki/Parinirvana "Parinirvana") of [Siddhartha Gautama](/wiki/Siddhartha_Gautama "Siddhartha Gautama"), the historical Buddha.[[47]](#cite_note-UNDay-47) Observed on the full moon of the month of [Vaisakha](/wiki/Vaisakha "Vaisakha") (April–May), Vesak is marked by visits to [temples](/wiki/Buddhist_temple "Buddhist temple"), offering flowers and lamps, chanting, meditation, and acts of compassion and generosity.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Theravāda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Theravāda")]

In the [Theravāda](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") tradition, several full-moon festivals commemorate major events in the Buddha's life and teachings, as well as milestones in the monastic year. [Magha Puja](/wiki/Magha_Puja "Magha Puja") (Sangha Day), held on the full moon of [Magha](/wiki/Magha_(month) "Magha (month)") (February–March), commemorates the spontaneous gathering of 1,250 [arahants](/wiki/Arahant "Arahant") to hear the Buddha's sermon on harmony and discipline.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Devotees light candles, chant, and participate in meditation.

[Asalha Puja](/wiki/Asalha_Puja "Asalha Puja") (Dhamma Day), observed on the full moon of Asalha (July), marks the [Buddha's first sermon](/wiki/Dhammacakkappavattana_Sutta "Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta") at Deer Park in [Sarnath](/wiki/Sarnath "Sarnath"), known as the “Turning of the Wheel of Dharma.” It is a time for reflection on the [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths") and for making merit.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[Vassa](/wiki/Vassa "Vassa"), or the Rains Retreat, is a three-month monastic retreat beginning in July, during which [monks](/wiki/Bhikkhu "Bhikkhu") remain in [monasteries](/wiki/Vihara "Vihara") for meditation and study. At the end of the retreat, on [Pavarana Day](/wiki/Pav%C4%81ra%E1%B9%87%C4%81 "Pavāraṇā"), monks seek mutual forgiveness, and lay followers participate in the [Kathina](/wiki/Ka%E1%B9%ADhina "Kaṭhina") Ceremony, offering new robes and requisites to the [Sangha](/wiki/Sangha "Sangha").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Regional Theravāda observances include [Poson Poya](/wiki/Poson "Poson") in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), marking the introduction of Buddhism to the island by [Arahant Mahinda Thera](/wiki/Mahinda_(Buddhist_monk) "Mahinda (Buddhist monk)"), and the [Thadingyut Festival](/wiki/Thadingyut_Festival "Thadingyut Festival") of Lights in [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), which celebrates the Buddha's descent from [Tavatimsa Heaven](/wiki/Tr%C4%81yastri%E1%B9%83%C5%9Ba "Trāyastriṃśa").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

#### Mahāyāna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Mahāyāna")]

[Mahāyāna Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") celebrates a number of festivals that emphasize the Buddha's spiritual milestones and the practice of compassion for all beings. [Bodhi Day](/wiki/Bodhi_Day "Bodhi Day"), held on 8 December, commemorates the Buddha's enlightenment under the [Bodhi Tree](/wiki/Bodhi_Tree "Bodhi Tree") in [Bodh Gaya](/wiki/Bodh_Gaya "Bodh Gaya"). Devotees meditate, study [sutras](/wiki/Sutra "Sutra"), and make offerings of tea, candles, and simple food.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

[Parinirvana Day](/wiki/Parinirvana_Day "Parinirvana Day") (Nirvana Day), observed on 15 February, marks the Buddha's final passing into [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana "Nirvana"). It is a time for meditation on [impermanence](/wiki/Impermanence_(Buddhism) "Impermanence (Buddhism)") and for reading the *[Mahāparinirvana Sūtra](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na_Mah%C4%81parinirv%C4%81%E1%B9%87a_S%C5%ABtra "Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra")*.[[54]](#cite_note-54) [Ullambana](/wiki/Ullambana "Ullambana"), also known as [Yulanpen](/wiki/Yulanpen "Yulanpen") in China and [Obon](/wiki/Obon "Obon") in Japan, is held in July or August to honor ancestors and relieve the suffering of spirits. Inspired by the legend of [Maudgalyayana](/wiki/Maudgalyayana "Maudgalyayana") saving his mother, people make offerings, light lanterns, and chant sutras.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In Japan, the [Hana Matsuri](/wiki/Buddha%27s_Birthday "Buddha's Birthday") or “Flower Festival” on 8 April celebrates the Buddha's birth, when images of the infant Buddha are bathed in sweet tea. In China, the [Laba Festival](/wiki/Laba_Festival "Laba Festival"), observed on the 8th day of the 12th lunar month (December–January), commemorates the Buddha's enlightenment with the preparation of *[Laba porridge](/wiki/Laba_congee "Laba congee")* and temple offerings.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### Vajrayāna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Vajrayāna")]

In [Vajrayāna](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana"), especially in [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and the Himalayan regions, festivals blend spiritual practice with rich cultural traditions. [Losar](/wiki/Losar "Losar"), the Tibetan New Year celebrated in February–March, marks a period of purification, renewal, and joy. Families clean their homes, perform rituals, and participate in community dances and celebrations.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Saga Dawa Duchen, observed on the full moon of the fourth Tibetan month (May–June), commemorates the Buddha's birth, enlightenment, and [parinirvana](/wiki/Parinirvana "Parinirvana"). It is regarded as the holiest month in the Tibetan calendar, during which people undertake [pilgrimages](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage"), recite mantras, and engage in acts of generosity.[[58]](#cite_note-auto2-58) Chökhor Düchen, celebrated on the fourth day of the sixth Tibetan month (July–August), honors the “Turning of the Wheel of Dharma,” the Buddha's first teaching of the [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths"). Devotees engage in meditation, rituals, and Dharma recitations.[[58]](#cite_note-auto2-58)

## Persecution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhists&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Persecution")]

Main articles: [Persecution of Buddhists](/wiki/Persecution_of_Buddhists "Persecution of Buddhists") and [Persecution of Buddhists in pre-Islamic Iran](/wiki/Persecution_of_Buddhists_in_pre-Islamic_Iran "Persecution of Buddhists in pre-Islamic Iran")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/The_end_of_Buddhist_Monks%2C_A.D._1193.jpg/250px-The_end_of_Buddhist_Monks%2C_A.D._1193.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_end_of_Buddhist_Monks,_A.D._1193.jpg)

The image, in the chapter on India in *Hutchison's Story of the Nations* edited by [James Meston](/wiki/James_Meston,_1st_Baron_Meston "James Meston, 1st Baron Meston"), depicts the Muslim Turkic general [Muhammad Bakhtiyar Khilji](/wiki/Muhammad_Bakhtiyar_Khalji "Muhammad Bakhtiyar Khalji")'s massacre of Buddhist monks in Bihar. Khaliji destroyed the [Nalanda](/wiki/Nalanda_mahavihara "Nalanda mahavihara") and [Vikramashila](/wiki/Vikramashila "Vikramashila") universities during his raids across North Indian plains, massacring many Buddhist and [Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") scholars.[[59]](#cite_note-Sanyal_130–1-59)

Buddhists have faced persecution at various points in history, often due to political, religious, or ethnic conflicts. In [India](/wiki/India "India"), Buddhism declined after the 12th century, partly due to invasions by [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") rulers and the destruction of [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") and [universities](/wiki/Nalanda_University "Nalanda University") such as [Nalanda](/wiki/Nalanda_mahavihara "Nalanda mahavihara") and [Vikramashila](/wiki/Vikramashila "Vikramashila").[[60]](#cite_note-60) In [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), the [Chinese invasion](/wiki/Chinese_invasion_of_Tibet "Chinese invasion of Tibet") of 1950 led to the suppression of religious institutions, imprisonment of monks, and destruction of monasteries, causing a large-scale diaspora of [Tibetan Buddhists](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

During the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") regime in [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") (1975–1979), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") was targeted as a symbol of the old society; monks were defrocked, temples destroyed, and religious practice banned, resulting in the deaths of thousands of monks.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In modern times, Buddhists in countries like [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and parts of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") face discrimination and threats as minority communities.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Despite these challenges, Buddhist communities have often emphasized [nonviolence](/wiki/Nonviolence "Nonviolence"), resilience, and dialogue, reflecting core teachings of [Compassion](/wiki/Compassion "Compassion") and tolerance while seeking protection of religious freedom and cultural heritage.[[64]](#cite_note-64)
